content:
AccessExclusiveLock doesn’t negotiate. Our “quick migration” became a traffic jam.

6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AccessExclusiveLock blocks reads and writes. Everything waits. The migration was “just adding a column.”
Thursday 2:08 PM. Deploy pipeline ran ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'standard'. orders: 340 million rows, 1.2 TB.
Migration tool: Flyway. Timeout: none. Lock: AccessExclusive.
2:08 — lock requested.
2:09–847 active queries on orders. Lock queues.
2:11 — checkout API p99 8.4s. Error rate 6%.
2:31 — migration killed manually. Lock released. Checkout recovers in 40 seconds.
23 minutes frozen checkout. Zero rows migrated — lock never acquired before we killed it.
The lock queue nobody visualized
-- what we ran at 2:14 PM — too late
SELECT blocked_locks.pid AS blocked_pid,
blocking_locks.pid AS blocking_pid,
blocked_activity.query AS blocked_query,
blocking_activity.query AS blocking_query
FROM pg_catalog.pg_locks blocked_locks
JOIN pg_catalog.pg_stat_activity blocked_activity ON blocked_activity.pid = blocked_locks.pid
JOIN pg_catalog.pg_locks blocking_locks
ON blocking_locks.locktype = blocked_locks.locktype
AND blocking_locks.relation IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blocked_locks.relation
AND blocking_locks.pid != blocked_locks.pid
JOIN pg_catalog.pg_stat_activity blocking_activity ON blocking_activity.pid = blocking_locks.pid
WHERE NOT blocked_locks.granted;
-- 847 rows blocked behind pid 28471 (ALTER TABLE)One ALTER. 847 victims. Checkout included.
AccessExclusiveLock — what it blocks
Blocks: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, most DDL.
Does not block: nothing on that table matters — everything waits.
Quick column add on Postgres 11+ with DEFAULT constant: can be fast metadata-only. On 340M rows with concurrent traffic: still needs lock acquisition window. No window existed.
-- the migration
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'standard';
-- lock mode: AccessExclusiveLock
-- wait time at kill: 23 min 14 secTen migration rules we violate at our peril

• Never DDL on hot tables during peak — 2 PM Thursday is peak

• Check pg_stat_activity count before ALTER — threshold: < 20 active on table

• Use lock_timeout — fail fast, don't queue silently

• statement_timeout on migration session

• ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT with constant: verify Postgres version fast-path applies

• Large table: pg_repack or expand-contract pattern for non-metadata changes

• Blue-green schema: new table, backfill, swap — for zero-downtime requirements

• Migration window in change calendar — blocked same day as marketing email blast (we ignored)

• Kill switch: on-call authorized to pg_cancel_backend migration pid at SEV2

• Rehearse on staging with production-scale row count — we had 4M rows in staging → PostgreSQL War Room — lock contention diagnosis tree, pg_blocking_pids, emergency queries. Minute two reading, not minute fourteen.
lock_timeout — the four-line fix we didn’t have
SET lock_timeout = '3s';
SET statement_timeout = '30min';
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'standard';With lock_timeout = 3s: migration fails at 2:08:03 instead of queueing 23 minutes.
Failed migration → alert → reschedule to 3 AM maintenance window. Zero checkout impact.
Without lock_timeout: queue until customers notice.
→ Your Database Is Bleeding Money. The Incident Playbook. — lock contention section with pg_blocking_pids decision tree. Copy-paste when ALTER TABLE is the incident.
How we killed it at 2:31
-- find migration backend
SELECT pid, query, state, wait_event_type, wait_event
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE query ILIKE '%ALTER TABLE orders%';
-- pid 28471SELECT pg_cancel_backend(28471); -- insufficient
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(28471); -- lock released2:31:08 — terminate.
2:31:48 — checkout p99 220ms. Error rate 0.2%.
23 minutes for 40 seconds recovery once we stopped the migration.
Expand-contract — what we should have done
Week 1: ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier VARCHAR(20) — nullable, no default, off-peak, lock_timeout
Week 2: application writes tier on new orders
Week 3: backfill batch job, 10k rows/second, no DDL
Week 4: ALTER COLUMN SET DEFAULT 'standard' — metadata, brief lock
Thursday tried week 1–4 in one ALTER at peak. Compressed into 23 minutes outage.
Staging lie — 4M rows vs 340M rows
Staging migration: 1.2 seconds. Production estimate extrapolated: “should be fine.”
Lock acquisition time scales with concurrent query count, not row count alone. Staging had 3 concurrent queries. Production had 847.
Rule: staging load test with synthetic concurrent readers before hot-table DDL. Tool: pgbench + migration in parallel.
pgbench -c 50 -T 60 -f read_orders.sql &
psql -c "SET lock_timeout='3s'; ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN ..."
# staging with load: fails in 3s — correct signalChange calendar collision
2 PM Thursday: marketing blast email — 40% traffic spike scheduled.
Migration approved in pipeline — no calendar integration.
Fix: Flyway runs check change calendar API. Hot table + peak window = block merge.
847 queries weren’t random. They were the email campaign working.
Post-Thursday policy
→ Hot tables (orders, users, payments): DDL only 2-5 AM UTC maintenance → lock_timeout = 3s mandatory in migration tool config → On-call kill authority for blocking DDL during SEV2 → Staging rehearsal requires concurrent load, not empty table → Expand-contract template for any ADD COLUMN on > 100M rows
340 million rows don’t care about your “quick migration.” AccessExclusiveLock cares about 847 queries ahead of you.
Online schema change tools — when Flyway isn’t enough
We evaluated gh-ost and pg_repack for future hot-table DDL. Thursday convinced finance to fund migration tool budget.
Short term: lock_timeout + maintenance windows.
Medium term: gh-ost-style online DDL for ADD COLUMN on > 50M rows without AccessExclusive queue.
Thursday cost: $8,400 estimated checkout loss + 6 hours engineering. Tool license pays for itself once.
The marketing calendar integration
Flyway pre-migrate hook now calls change calendar API:
if hot_table_migration && peak_traffic_window; then
echo "BLOCKED: orders DDL during campaign window"
exit 1
fi2 PM Thursday would have failed CI. Migration runs 3 AM Friday. Zero customers notice column add.
Monitoring lock queue during migrations
We added migration session tag:
SET application_name = 'flyway_migration';Grafana alert: count(pg_locks WHERE NOT granted AND relation = orders.oid) > 10 during migration window → auto-kill migration pid.
Would have fired at 2:09 Thursday — 2 minutes into queue, not 23.
SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE application_name = 'flyway_migration'
AND query ILIKE '%ALTER TABLE%';Kill switch automated. On-call still has manual override. Automation catches what humans miss at minute 2.
Customer-facing error messages during lock wait
Checkout errors at 2:11: canceling statement due to lock timeout leaked to API response.
Bad UX. Worse: customers tweeted SQL errors.
Fix: app maps lock timeout to generic “try again” — never expose Postgres internals. Separate ticket from migration. Same incident, two wounds.
The 3 AM Friday migration that succeeded
Same ALTER TABLE. Same column. 3:07 AM Friday:
SET lock_timeout = '3s';
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN loyalty_tier VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'standard';
-- Time: 847ms
-- blocked queries at peak: 2847ms. Not 23 minutes. Not 847 blocked queries.
Change calendar clear. Marketing asleep. pg_stat_activity count on orders: 3.
Thursday taught the window. Friday executed the window. Column shipped. Zero tweets.
Flyway config — lock_timeout global now
# flyway.conf
flyway.postgresql.transactional.lock=false
flyway.initSql=SET lock_timeout='3s'; SET statement_timeout='30min';Every migration inherits timeouts. Cannot merge Flyway config without timeout lines — linter enforces.
Thursday engineer didn’t forget. Config didn’t exist. Different failure mode. Same customer pain.
Hot table registry — migrations require approval
orders, users, payments, sessions — registered hot tables. Any DDL requires:
→ Change ticket with maintenance window → Load test proof on staging with concurrent readers → DBA sign-off → lock_timeout verified in Flyway config
Thursday migration had ticket. Missing: window check, load test, timeout config. Three of four failures.
Registry would have blocked merge until four of four.
orders table stats — why 340M rows mattered
SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('orders')),
n_live_tup, n_dead_tup
FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE relname = 'orders';
-- 1.2 TB | 340000000 | 12000000Metadata-only ADD COLUMN on Postgres 14+ with constant default: often instant. Unless lock cannot acquire.
340M rows meant long-running queries held lock queue. Row count mattered indirectly. Concurrent query count mattered directly.
Postmortem — Thursday vs Friday migration comparison
→ Thursday 2 PM: 847 blocked queries, 23 min outage, migration killed manually → Friday 3 AM: 2 blocked queries, 847ms migration, success
Same DDL. Different window. Different config. Only Friday belonged in production.
Checkout error rate — the customer graph
2:08–2:31 — error rate 4% → 6% → killed migration.
2:31–2:35 — recovery to 0.3%.
23 minutes customer pain. $8,400 estimated. 3 AM Friday migration: zero customer-visible errors.
Window and timeout are free. Outages are not.

Froquiz database scenarios include lock queue triage — know when to pg_terminate_backend the migration before minute 23 of frozen checkout. → Froquiz
